title_en: The Abbasid Caliphate — Rise and Fall
content_en:
The Abbasid Caliphate (132–656 AH / 750–1258 CE), which replaced the Umayyad dynasty following the successful Abbasid revolution, represents the longest-lasting and in many ways the most intellectually and culturally productive period in Islamic civilization. Ibn Kathir's account of this era in al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah covers five centuries of Islamic history, from the first Abbasid Caliph al-Saffah through the catastrophic Mongol conquest that ended the Baghdad caliphate in 656 AH.

The Abbasid revolution was carried out in the name of restoring the rights of the Prophet's ﷺ family (Ahl al-Bayt) and correcting the perceived inequalities of Umayyad governance, particularly the marginalization of non-Arab Muslims (especially Persian converts) who had embraced Islam but been treated as second-class citizens within the Umayyad system. The Abbasid claim to legitimacy rested on their descent from al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's ﷺ uncle. The new capital, Baghdad (Madinat al-Salam — City of Peace), was founded by the Caliph al-Mansur in 145 AH (762 CE) as a purpose-built administrative center — a circular city of extraordinary geometric elegance and practical functionality that quickly became the largest city in the world outside China.

The golden age of the Abbasid Caliphate — associated particularly with the reigns of al-Mahdi, Harun al-Rashid (r. 170–193 AH / 786–809 CE), and al-Ma'mun (r. 198–218 AH / 813–833 CE) — was one of the greatest periods of intellectual achievement in human history. Under the patronage of these Caliphs and their courts, the Bayt al-Hikmah (House of Wisdom) in Baghdad became the world's leading center of learning — translating and engaging with the entire corpus of Greek philosophy, medicine, mathematics, and astronomy, while simultaneously developing original Arabic-Islamic contributions in algebra (al-Khwarizmi), medicine (Ibn Sina), philosophy (al-Kindi, al-Farabi), and the religious sciences. The scholars of hadith, jurisprudence, and Quranic exegesis who produced the foundational works of Islamic scholarship — al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Shafi'i, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and scores of others — lived and worked during the Abbasid era.

The political and military decline of the Abbasid Caliphate began gradually in the third Islamic century, as the Caliphs increasingly delegated real military and political power to Turkish military commanders (the ghulam system) who became de facto rulers while the Abbasid Caliph retained a largely ceremonial and religious legitimacy. By the fourth century AH, the empire had fragmented into numerous independent sultanates — the Buyid dynasty, the Samanids, the Fatimids, the Ghaznavids — each owing nominal allegiance to the Baghdad Caliphate while exercising full sovereignty.

The final chapter of the Abbasid Caliphate was written by the Mongols. In 656 AH (1258 CE), the Mongol forces of Hulagu Khan — grandson of Chinggis Khan — besieged and sacked Baghdad in an event that Islamic civilization has never entirely recovered its initial shock from. The last Abbasid Caliph, al-Musta'sim, was executed. The Bayt al-Hikmah and its priceless libraries were destroyed; hundreds of thousands of Muslims were killed. Ibn Kathir, writing approximately a century after the Mongol sack, treats this event with great solemnity — it was, in his words, one of the greatest calamities to befall Islam since the beginning of the religion. Yet even in this catastrophe, he perceives divine wisdom: the Mongols who conquered the Islamic east were themselves converted to Islam within a generation, and the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt — preserving the remnant of the Abbasid caliphate in Cairo — defeated the Mongols at Ain Jalut in 658 AH, halting the westward Mongol expansion and preserving the Islamic world of North Africa and the Levant. History, in Ibn Kathir's vision, is always ultimately in the hands of Allah.
